questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT one of the deck's three levels of testing?
questions.explanation: Unit, integration, and system are the three LEVELS; differential testing is a cross-cutting kind, not a level. (Software Testing p.8)

questions.stem: Which of these is NOT a black-box design technique?

options.text: Deriving tests from the domain to range spec

questions.explanation: Branch coverage is a white-box coverage criterion; equivalence partitioning and boundary-value analysis are black-box techniques derived from the spec. (Software Testing p.12, p.23)

questions.stem: What does the deck's title slide say the goal of software testing is?

options.text: To make software fail (on purpose, in the lab)

questions.explanation: The title framing is 'Software Testing: How to make software fail!' - surfacing defects in the lab, not in production. (Software Testing p.1)

questions.stem: What does the acronym DRY stand for, cited as a refactoring goal in TDD?
questions.explanation: DRY = Don't Repeat Yourself; refactoring removes duplication. (Software Testing p.48)

questions.stem: To assert that an expected value matches an actual one, which JUnit assertion do you reach for?
questions.explanation: assertEquals(expected, actual) checks equality of the two values. (Software Testing p.53)

questions.stem: Which JUnit assertion forces an unconditional failure when reached?
questions.explanation: fail() unconditionally fails the test where it is reached (used in the try/fail/catch idiom). (Software Testing p.53, p.55)

questions.stem: When the deck says to 'test under the UI', what practice is it prescribing?

options.text: Test the pixels of the GUI
Call the domain/application layer directly, bypassing the widgets

questions.explanation: Testing under the UI means exercising domain logic directly, not through GUI widgets. (Software Testing p.29)

questions.stem: At any given moment, TDD has you focused on how many tests?
questions.explanation: TDD discipline: one test at a time. (Software Testing p.39)

questions.stem: Arrange the coverage criteria from weakest to strongest. Which ordering is right?
questions.explanation: By strength: statement subset branch subset path. (Software Testing p.13)

questions.stem: A bug report says the system crashes only when two already-working modules exchange data because one sends metres and the other expects feet. Which test LEVEL is designed to catch this seam defect?
questions.explanation: The defect lives in the seam between two independently-correct modules, which is exactly what integration testing exercises; unit tests pass on each side, and stress/statement coverage target other concerns. (Software Testing p.8)

questions.stem: Testers report that a function returns the right answer for 5, 50, and 500 but throws on the largest representable integer. Which black-box technique would most directly have predicted that the failing case needed its own test?
questions.explanation: Defects cluster at the edges of value ranges, so boundary-value analysis flags the maximum-integer edge as its own case; random testing only stumbles on it by luck and the others target different goals. (Software Testing p.12)

questions.stem: A defect surfaces only after thousands of concurrent users push the server past its rated load. Which testing kind is meant to find this?
questions.explanation: Stress testing pushes a system past expected load/volume/rate to find where it breaks; the others check single-input correctness or component isolation. (Software Testing p.8)

questions.stem: A crash only appears when a user clicks a sequence of toolbar buttons in a Swing editor, and the team's automated tests never reproduce it. Which strategy does the deck recommend to make most of that logic cheaply testable?

options.text: Test under the UI by calling the domain layer directly
Increase statement coverage of the widget classes
Disable assertions to speed the tests

questions.explanation: GUI inputs (clicks/events) and state outputs are brittle to drive, so the deck's cure is to pull the logic out and test the domain layer directly under the UI. (Software Testing p.29)

questions.stem: A team can only reproduce an error-handling bug when the disk is full, which almost never happens in the lab. Which technique deterministically triggers the failure path?
questions.explanation: Fault injection forces a dependency to fail on demand (e.g. wrap open() to fail 1% of calls) so unhappy paths can be tested deterministically. (Software Testing p.31)

questions.stem: A unit test for borrowBook would otherwise reach out to a live OverdueService over the network, making it slow and flaky. What is the correct fix to keep it a fast unit test?

options.text: Run the test only when the network is up
Convert it into a system test
Replace the OverdueService with a mock/stub so the test never hits the network
Remove the OverdueService dependency from production

questions.explanation: When execution leaves the method under test you have a dependency; replace it with a test double so the test stays an isolated single code-path. (TestLab1 p.1)

questions.stem: A reviewer wants a single test that simply confirms a method throws TooManyBooksException, with no need to inspect the exception object. Which JUnit idiom is most concise?
questions.explanation: The annotation form @Test(expected=...) is the concise idiom when only the exception type matters. (Software Testing p.55)

questions.stem: A new requirement says 'a user may borrow at most 10 books.' Following TDD, what is the FIRST thing you write?

options.text: A failing test that borrows 10 books then expects an exception on the 11th
The full borrowBook implementation with the guard
A refactor of the existing borrowBook

questions.explanation: TDD is test-first: write the failing (red) test for the alternative scenario before any production code. (Software Testing p.45)

questions.stem: During code review you find a JHotDraw figure class marked final that the team tried to mock, and the mock fails to compile. What does the deck say to do?

options.text: Switch the test to a manual GUI script
Program to the interface, since Mockito cannot mock final classes
Disable assertions so the mock works

questions.explanation: Mockito cannot mock final, anonymous, or primitive types; the design implication is to program to interfaces. (Software Testing p.67)

questions.stem: A team's suite passes on module A and module B in isolation but the assembled product still fails. Which fault-location does the Mars Orbiter lesson point you toward checking?

options.text: The specification/interface agreement between the modules

questions.explanation: Mars Orbiter was a spec/interface unit mismatch invisible to each side's unit tests; check the contract between modules. (Software Testing p.9)

questions.stem: You want to test the unhappy path where a file write fails on the 101st call. Which approach matches the deck's my-open() example?

options.text: Fill the real disk before the test
Use boundary-value analysis on the filename
Inject a wrapper that succeeds 100 times then fails 1% of calls

questions.explanation: The deck's fault-injection example wraps open() with my-open() that succeeds 100 times then fails 1% of calls. (Software Testing p.31)

questions.stem: A tester only needs a canned return value from a pricing service and does not care how many times it was called. Which double is the most appropriate, lightest choice?
questions.explanation: A stub returns predefined data and is the lightest, most static double; use it when you only need a canned answer with no call verification. (Software Testing p.60)

questions.stem: A test's whole point is to confirm that the SUT actually called notifyListeners() exactly once. Which double is appropriate?
questions.explanation: When the interaction itself is under test, use a mock and verify() to record and assert the call. (Software Testing p.59)

questions.stem: Which of these is NOT one of the four fragile-test sensitivities the deck enumerates?
questions.explanation: The four sensitivities are interface, behavior, data, and context; 'coverage sensitivity' is invented. (Software Testing p.25)

questions.stem: Which item is NOT on the deck's 'Creating Testable Software' checklist?

options.text: Maximize use of global mutable state

questions.explanation: The checklist forbids global-variable swaps; maximizing global mutable state is the opposite of testability. (Software Testing p.14)

questions.stem: Which is NOT a valid use of a Java assertion, per the three rules?

options.text: Checking an invariant that should never be false
Failing early near a programmer bug

questions.explanation: Rule 1: assertions are not for error handling; expected bad input gets an exception, not an assertion. (Software Testing p.18)

questions.stem: Which construct can Mockito NOT mock?
questions.explanation: Mockito cannot mock final classes (also anonymous classes and primitive types); interfaces and ordinary classes are fine. (Software Testing p.67)

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT a stated benefit of TDD in the deck?

options.text: Helps design the system from the caller's view
It proves the program is fully correct
Produces a testable system as a by-product

questions.explanation: TDD never proves full correctness (Dijkstra still holds); the real benefits are coverage and design. (Software Testing p.49)

questions.stem: Which is NOT a property of a good unit test as described/implied by the deck's isolation strategy?

options.text: It depends on a live external server for realism

questions.explanation: A good unit test isolates the method by replacing dependencies with doubles; depending on a live server breaks isolation and repeatability. (TestLab1 p.1)

questions.stem: Which statement is NOT true about Java assertions?

options.text: They are enabled by default at runtime
Disabling them lets the program keep running past a violation
They should have no side effects

questions.explanation: Java assertions are OFF by default and must be enabled with -ea; the claim that they are on by default is false. (Software Testing p.16)

questions.stem: Which is NOT a member of the xUnit-related toolset the deck lists?
questions.explanation: The deck lists xUnit, CppUnit, JUnit, Mockito, Sikuli Script, and DbUnit; JaCoCo is not among them. (Software Testing p.56)

questions.stem: Which is NOT a way the deck says to create a Mockito mock?

options.text: Subclassing the class manually with new

questions.explanation: Mocks are created via Mockito.mock(...) or @Mock (with initMocks); hand-subclassing is not the deck's mechanism. (Software Testing p.65)

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT one of the three TDD cycle steps?
questions.explanation: The cycle is red, green, refactor; 'regress' is not a TDD step. (Software Testing p.39)

questions.stem: Which is NOT a JUnit lifecycle annotation discussed in the deck?
questions.explanation: The JUnit 4 annotations covered are @Before/@After and @BeforeClass/@AfterClass; @AfterEach is JUnit 5 vocabulary not used here. (Software Testing p.52)

questions.stem: What most precisely distinguishes a mock from a stub?

options.text: A stub wraps a real object; a mock does not
A mock records and verifies interactions; a stub only returns canned data
A mock cannot return values; a stub can
There is no difference; the terms are interchangeable

questions.explanation: A stub answers queries with predefined data (no verification); a mock additionally records calls so you can verify the interaction. (Software Testing p.59-60)

questions.stem: What distinguishes a spy from a plain mock?

options.text: A spy cannot be verified; a mock can
A spy is lighter and more static than a stub
A spy wraps a real object and delegates unspecified calls to it; a mock is a full stand-in
A spy can only be used with final classes

questions.explanation: A spy is a partial mock around a real object: unspecified calls run the real method, whereas a mock replaces the object entirely. (Software Testing p.72-73)

questions.stem: How do statement and branch coverage differ?

options.text: Statement coverage is strictly stronger than branch coverage
They are identical for code without loops
Branch coverage also requires every decision's true AND false outcome; statement coverage only requires each line to run

questions.explanation: Statement coverage runs every line at least once; branch coverage additionally forces both outcomes of every decision, making it stronger. (Software Testing p.11, +[Raj13])

questions.stem: How does equivalence partitioning differ from boundary-value analysis?

options.text: Partitioning picks one representative per class; boundary analysis tests the edges of those classes
Partitioning tests edges; boundary analysis picks one representative per class
Both test only the midpoint of each class
Partitioning is white-box; boundary analysis is black-box

questions.explanation: Equivalence partitioning tests one representative per class of equivalent inputs; boundary-value analysis then tests the edges where defects cluster. (Software Testing p.11-12)

questions.stem: What is the core difference between black-box and white-box testing?

options.text: Black-box derives tests from the source code; white-box from the spec
Black-box derives tests from the spec/interface; white-box derives them from the source-code structure

questions.explanation: Black-box uses only the spec/interface (domain to range); white-box uses code structure to target statements/branches/paths. (Software Testing p.8)

questions.stem: How does an assertion differ from an exception, per TestLab1?

options.text: An assertion lets the program continue; an exception stops it
Both are used for expected bad input
An assertion should stop the program; an exception should let it continue
Exceptions check invariants; assertions handle user errors

questions.explanation: TestLab1: an assertion (a 'should never happen' check) stops the program, while an exception lets it continue. (TestLab1 p.1)

questions.stem: How do @Before and @BeforeClass differ in frequency?

options.text: @Before runs once; @BeforeClass runs before each test
Both run before every test method
Both run exactly once per suite
@Before runs before EACH test; @BeforeClass runs ONCE before all tests in the class

questions.explanation: @Before runs before each test method (fresh fixture); @BeforeClass is static and runs once for the whole class (expensive shared setup). (Software Testing p.52)

questions.stem: How does traditional acceptance testing differ from the Agile mode the deck describes?

options.text: Traditional is manual, by the customer, after delivery; Agile is automatic, created before implementation
Traditional is automatic and pre-implementation; Agile is manual and post-delivery
Both are manual and run after delivery
Both are automated and written by developers only

questions.explanation: Traditionally acceptance tests are manual, customer-run, after delivery; in Agile they are automatic (JUnit/Fit) and written before the story is implemented. (Software Testing p.78)

questions.stem: How does manual testing compare with automated testing in the deck's verdict?

options.text: Manual is cheap and supports regression; automated is expensive
Manual is expensive and cannot be run often; automated is cheap and enables regression
Both are equally cheap to run repeatedly
Automated tests cannot give immediate feedback

questions.explanation: The deck says manual tests should be avoided because they are expensive and can't run often; automated tests are cheap and enable regression. (Software Testing p.81)

questions.stem: Which pair correctly matches a test-double term to its defining trait?

options.text: Stub = records + verifies; Mock = canned data only
Stub = wraps real object; Mock = simplified working implementation
Stub = canned data, no verification; Mock = records + verifies
Stub = primitive type; Mock = final class

questions.explanation: A stub returns predefined data without verification; a mock records calls and verifies interactions. (Software Testing p.59-60)

questions.stem: A test deliberately enqueues past capacity to force getTail() back to 0, using knowledge of the internal 'if (tail == max) tail = 0;' line. How is this test best classified?
questions.explanation: Knowing the internal wrap-around line and driving that path is white-box, path-targeted testing. (Software Testing p.13)

questions.stem: A test was written purely from 'sqrt: R+ -> R+' and the domain to range table, with no view of the implementation. Which kind of test is it?
questions.explanation: Deriving tests only from the spec/interface (domain to range) is black-box testing. (Software Testing p.23)

questions.stem: A test borrows 10 books, then asserts a TooManyBooksException on the 11th. Which 'idea for tests' category does this belong to?

options.text: Acceptance test from an alternative use-case scenario

questions.explanation: The alternative scenario (already has 10 books -> error) is a use-case-driven acceptance test idea. (Software Testing p.40, p.45)

questions.stem: A test breaks every time a developer reorders the parameters of a method, even though behaviour is unchanged. Which fragile-test sensitivity does this identify?
questions.explanation: Coupling to the shape of the API (signatures, parameter order) is interface sensitivity. (Software Testing p.25)

questions.stem: A test fails after the team legitimately changes a feature's behaviour, and the breakage is actually correct. Which sensitivity is this?
questions.explanation: When a test breaks because behaviour legitimately changed, that is behavior sensitivity (the breakage may be correct). (Software Testing p.26)

questions.stem: A test only passes when run before another test that empties a shared collection; run alone it fails. Which sensitivity does this symptom point to?
questions.explanation: Dependence on test ordering/environment is context sensitivity. (Software Testing p.28)

questions.stem: A method's test sets up DI so Spring injects the real DataModelService bean and then asserts on it. Which testing arrangement is this?

options.text: Integration of the DI container into JUnit (Spring + JUnit)

questions.explanation: The @RunWith(SpringJUnit4ClassRunner) + @Autowired pattern wires real/test beans via DI and asserts on the injected bean. (Software Testing p.76)

questions.stem: A maintainer ports a feature to a new release and wants a cheap, automatic oracle to confirm the new code behaves like the old. Which technique fits, using the previous release as the reference?
questions.explanation: Differential testing runs the SUT against a reference implementation and asserts the outputs match; in maintenance the previous release is a free oracle. (Software Testing p.8)

questions.stem: A scheduled job mis-decides 'is the book overdue?' only around midnight, and the bug is nearly impossible to reproduce on demand. Which design change makes the behaviour testable?

options.text: Add a Thread.sleep to slow the test down
Run the test only at midnight
Increase the number of random inputs
Inject the clock behind an interface and substitute a controllable fake in tests

questions.explanation: Time-dependent code is context-sensitive; the standard mitigation is to inject the clock so 'now' is whatever the test dictates. (Software Testing p.32)

questions.stem: A bug appears only because a test relies on a specific row in a shared live database that another test mutates, producing intermittent red runs. Which fragile-test sensitivity is this and what is the cure?

options.text: Data sensitivity; own and control your test data

questions.explanation: Coupling to specific data/state is data sensitivity; the cure is to own and control the fixture rather than share mutable live data. (Software Testing p.27)

questions.stem: Your CI build goes red on the build server but green on your laptop because the server runs in a German locale where the decimal separator differs. Which sensitivity is at work?
questions.explanation: Coupling to environment such as locale, time, or filesystem is context sensitivity. (Software Testing p.28)

questions.stem: You must verify a method throws IllegalArgumentException AND assert that the exception's message contains the offending value. Which idiom fits?

options.text: try { m(); fail(); } catch (IllegalArgumentException e) { /* assert on e */ }

questions.explanation: Use the try/fail/catch idiom when you must inspect the caught exception's contents; the annotation form cannot assert on the exception object. (Software Testing p.55)

questions.stem: A maintainer faces a tangled, untested JHotDraw method they need to refactor. Following the TDD/change-process linkage, which step comes first?

options.text: Refactor first, then add tests if time allows
Disable assertions to avoid noise during refactoring
Run a stress test on the method
Write a passing test suite to act as a safety net before refactoring

questions.explanation: Refactoring is only safe with a good test suite; green tests are the licence to refactor (the mandatory third TDD step). (Software Testing p.48)

questions.stem: A unit test for a figure's bounds passes, but you want every operation in the test to also re-validate the figure's invariant automatically. What does the queue example do that you should copy?

options.text: Wrap the object so checkRep runs after each operation (e.g. in setUp)

questions.explanation: The queue's setUp wraps the object in a CheckRepWrapper so every operation is validated against the representation invariant. (Software Testing p.52)

questions.stem: Which statement does NOT correctly describe a test oracle?

options.text: The oracle decides pass or fail
The oracle generates the test inputs
A hard-coded expected value can be the oracle
A reference implementation can serve as the oracle

questions.explanation: The oracle judges 'Outputs OK?'; it decides pass/fail and can be an expected value or reference impl, but it does not generate inputs. (Software Testing p.7)

questions.stem: Which claim about coverage criteria is NOT correct?

options.text: Statement coverage is the weakest criterion
Branch coverage requires both true and false outcomes of each decision
100% path coverage proves the program correct
Path coverage is usually infeasible for loops

questions.explanation: Even 100% path coverage does not prove correctness (Dijkstra's presence-not-absence); the other three statements are true. (Software Testing p.6)

questions.stem: Which correctly contrasts branch coverage with path coverage?

options.text: Path coverage is weaker than branch coverage
Branch coverage requires every independent path; path coverage only each branch
They are the same once loops are present
Branch coverage takes each branch at least once; path coverage requires every combination of branches

questions.explanation: Branch coverage needs each branch taken once; path coverage needs every combination of branches (every independent path), which is strongest. (Software Testing p.13, +[Raj13])

questions.stem: Differential testing and regression testing both compare against a 'reference.' What is the key difference?

options.text: Differential testing compares the SUT to a separate reference implementation; regression re-runs the SUT's own prior suite to detect new breakage
Both compare to a separate implementation
Regression compares two independent implementations; differential re-runs old tests

questions.explanation: Differential testing uses another implementation as the oracle; regression re-runs the existing automated suite after each change to catch newly introduced bugs. (Software Testing p.8, p.81)

questions.stem: Order the three test doubles from lightest/most static to most powerful, per the deck.
questions.explanation: The stub is the lightest/most static; the spy wraps a real object; the mock is the most powerful and flexible. (Software Testing p.59-60, p.72)

questions.stem: A fake and a stub are both test doubles. What is the key difference noted for exam completeness?

options.text: A fake only returns canned values; a stub is a full implementation
A fake records and verifies calls; a stub does not exist
A fake is a working but simplified implementation (e.g. in-memory DB); a stub just returns canned values
They are the same as a spy

questions.explanation: A fake is a working but simplified implementation such as an in-memory database; a stub merely supplies predefined responses. (Software Testing p.60, +[Raj13])

questions.stem: A test passes book==null to borrowBook and checks the borrowed count did not change. Which category from the deck's 'ideas for tests' is this?

options.text: An acceptance test from a main scenario
A code experiment with no assertion

questions.explanation: Probing book==null to pin down a possible defect is a defect/boundary test. (Software Testing p.47, p.40)

questions.stem: Given the assertion 'assert result > 0;' placed just before returning from sqrt, what is it best classified as?
questions.explanation: An executable check of a property that must always hold (a real square root is positive) is an invariant/postcondition assertion. (Software Testing p.18)

questions.stem: A test wraps the real UserProvider and only overrides one method while letting the rest run normally. Which double has been used?
questions.explanation: A spy wraps a real object and delegates unspecified calls to it, overriding only specified methods. (Software Testing p.73)

questions.stem: A team tests all logic by driving the GUI directly with click scripts. What consequence does the deck predict?

options.text: Brittle, slow tests because GUI inputs/outputs are clicks and app states

questions.explanation: GUI inputs are clicks/events and outputs are app states, which are brittle and slow to test; the cure is to test under the UI. (Software Testing p.30)

questions.stem: A maintainer skips the refactor step in every TDD cycle. What is the predictable consequence?

options.text: The code becomes impossible to mock
Duplication and technical debt accumulate over time

questions.explanation: Refactoring is the mandatory third TDD step; skipping it lets duplication/debt build up. (Software Testing p.48)

questions.stem: Assertions are never enabled with -ea in any environment. What is the consequence for a checkRep-based design?

options.text: The invariant checks run nowhere and provide no safety net
The checks run twice as often

questions.explanation: Java assertions are off by default; without -ea, checkRep/invariant checks never execute. (Software Testing p.16)

questions.stem: To get adequate coverage of an infinite input domain with a finite, justified set of tests, which technique is MOST appropriate?
questions.explanation: Equivalence partitioning yields a finite, justified test set over an infinite domain by testing one representative per class. (Software Testing p.12)

questions.stem: A figure's invariant 'bounds width >= 0' must be checked on every operation during tests, with minimal noise. Which mechanism is BEST?

options.text: An exception thrown on every call
A checkRep() built from assertions, invoked via the test fixture

questions.explanation: A checkRep() of assertions invoked from the fixture validates the invariant on every operation. (Software Testing p.19)

questions.stem: To deterministically exercise an I/O error path that rarely occurs naturally, which technique is the BEST choice?
questions.explanation: Fault injection forces the dependency to fail on demand, deterministically exercising rare error paths. (Software Testing p.31)

questions.stem: Someone says 'statement, branch, and path coverage are independent metrics with no relationship.' Correct them.

options.text: They form a subset/strength hierarchy: statement is weakest, then branch, then path
Path coverage is the weakest of the three

questions.explanation: The criteria are ordered by increasing strength: statement subset branch subset path. (Software Testing p.13, +[Raj13])

questions.stem: Someone believes 'the oracle problem is solved as long as the test runs without crashing.' Why is this wrong?

options.text: Running without crashing says nothing about whether the output is correct; you still need an oracle to judge correctness
The oracle only matters for GUI tests
Crashes are the only failures that matter

questions.explanation: The oracle decides 'Outputs OK?'; a test that merely doesn't crash hasn't checked correctness. (Software Testing p.7)

questions.stem: A developer claims 'TDD means write all the tests first, then all the code.' Why is this a misconception?

options.text: That is exactly what TDD prescribes
TDD is one failing test at a time, then minimal code to pass, then refactor - not all-tests-then-all-code
TDD writes code first, tests later

questions.explanation: TDD discipline: one test at a time, implement only enough to pass, then refactor. (Software Testing p.39)

questions.stem: A teammate believes 'because each module's unit tests pass, the integrated system is guaranteed to work.' Why is this false?

options.text: Unit tests do guarantee integration success
System failures are always platform bugs
Integration defects (e.g. unit mismatches like Mars Orbiter) live between modules and pass each side's unit tests

questions.explanation: Mars Orbiter shows a spec/interface mismatch invisible to each module's internally-consistent unit tests. (Software Testing p.9)

questions.stem: In the deck's Borrow-Book walkthrough, what is the correct order of the first four moves?

options.text: Write failing main-scenario test -> minimal borrowBook -> failing alternative test -> add the size>=10 guard
Add the guard -> write tests -> refactor -> implement

questions.explanation: The deck: red main test, minimal add() to pass, red alternative test, then the >=10 guard to pass. (Software Testing p.42-46)

questions.stem: Your regression run lights up red in one spot. What does the walkthrough tell you to do as the immediate next move?

options.text: Triage the fault via the decision tree: SUT? test? spec? platform?

questions.explanation: A red regression triggers fault triage across SUT, acceptance test, spec, and platform before fixing. (Software Testing p.9, p.81)

questions.stem: After the main Borrow-Book scenario is green, what is the next move the deck makes before touching the implementation again?

options.text: Write a new failing test for the 'already has 10 books' alternative scenario
Write a defect test for null

questions.explanation: The deck writes the failing alternative-scenario test next, which then forces the guard implementation. (Software Testing p.45)

questions.stem: A JHotDraw class you need to substitute is declared final. What does the case-study guidance say?

options.text: Mock it anyway with a spy
Switch to a manual test only
Recall Mockito cannot mock final classes; program to the interface instead

questions.explanation: Mockito's limitation on final classes means you should program to the interface to keep it mockable. (Software Testing p.67)

questions.stem: For Swing/JUnit work on a JHotDraw feature, which JUnit version does TestLab1 specifically recommend and why?

options.text: JUnit 4, because Swing and JUnit extensions often work best with it
Any version; it makes no difference

questions.explanation: TestLab1 notes Swing and JUnit extensions often work best with JUnit 4. (TestLab1 p.1)

questions.stem: Why should the authorship of JUnit matter when you are specifically testing JHotDraw?

options.text: Erich Gamma co-authored both JUnit and JHotDraw, so JHotDraw's interface-based design is naturally testable/mockable
JHotDraw ships with its own test framework
JUnit was written specifically for Swing

questions.explanation: Gamma (a Design Patterns author) co-wrote JUnit and JHotDraw; the pattern-based interfaces make it ideal for mocks/stubs. (Software Testing p.51)

questions.stem: Read this snippet: when(test.getUniqueId()).thenReturn(43); What does it do?

options.text: Stubs getUniqueId() on the mock to return 43 whenever called
Throws if getUniqueId() returns anything but 43

questions.explanation: when(...).thenReturn(...) stubs the method to return the given value; it does not verify calls. (Software Testing p.70)

questions.stem: In the EnqueueTest, after q.enqueue(1); q.enqueue(2); q.enqueue(3) on a Queue(2), why does the test assert q.getTail() == 0?

options.text: Because three enqueues always reset the tail
The ring buffer wrapped: tail hit max and reset to 0 via 'if (tail == max) tail = 0;'

questions.explanation: The wrap-around boundary 'if (tail == max) tail = 0;' resets tail to 0, which the test asserts. (Software Testing p.52)

questions.stem: Read this checkRep fragment: 'if (getTail() == getHead()) assert (getSize()==0) || (getSize()==getMax());' What invariant does it encode?

options.text: When head meets tail, the queue is either empty or completely full
Size must equal max at all times

questions.explanation: In a ring buffer, head==tail is ambiguous between empty and full, so the invariant asserts size is 0 or max. (Software Testing p.19)

questions.stem: Read this fixture: @Before public void setUp(){ q = new CheckRepWrapper(new Queue(2)); }. What is the intent of wrapping in CheckRepWrapper?

options.text: Every operation validates the queue against its representation invariant
It records all method calls for verify()

questions.explanation: The CheckRepWrapper runs checkRep after operations so each test validates the invariant. (Software Testing p.52)

questions.stem: Read: @Spy private UserProvider userProvider = new ConsumerUserProvider(); What behaviour does this set up?

options.text: A full mock that returns null for everything
A stub returning canned data only
A partial mock wrapping the real ConsumerUserProvider; unspecified calls run the real method
A dummy that is never used

questions.explanation: @Spy wraps the real object; calls delegate to it unless explicitly stubbed. (Software Testing p.73)

questions.stem: Read: assertFalse(borrowedBooks.contains(book)); ... user.borrowBook(book); ... assertTrue(borrowedBooks.contains(book)); What pattern and roles are shown?

options.text: A differential test against a reference
Fault injection of the book list

questions.explanation: This asserts a precondition, performs the action, then asserts the postcondition - Arrange-Act-Assert. (Software Testing p.43)

questions.stem: The guide links the impossibility of exhaustive path coverage back to the p.5 theoretical result. What real-world cause makes that connection hold?

options.text: Loops explode the path count, so exhaustive paths are unreachable just as a perfect test suite is theoretically impossible

questions.explanation: Loop-driven path explosion makes full path coverage practically unreachable, mirroring the theoretical impossibility of a perfect suite. (Software Testing p.13, p.5)

questions.stem: A spec gives equivalence classes positive, zero, negative, NaN, infinity, overflow for a float function. What does this directly tell the tester?

options.text: That only the positive class needs testing
Which tests must exist - one representative per class
That floats and reals behave identically
That no boundary tests are needed

questions.explanation: The spec's equivalence classes dictate which black-box tests must exist, one per class. (Software Testing p.23)

questions.stem: In the deck's layered strategy, record-and-play GUI checks are used only sparingly near the top. Which characteristic of them justifies that limited, high-level use?

options.text: It is faster and more reliable than unit tests
It replaces the need for unit tests
It is more fragile (context/data sensitive) and slower, so it covers less and is used sparingly
It is the only way to test domain logic

questions.explanation: GUI replay is brittle and slow, so the bulk of logic should be tested under the UI and replay used sparingly on top. (Software Testing p.30)

questions.stem: The Spring + JUnit test uses assertThat(ml, instanceOf(...)) and assertThat(ml.isValid(""), is(true)). Which library provides these matchers?
questions.explanation: assertThat with is()/instanceOf() are Hamcrest matchers for readable assertions. (Software Testing p.76)

questions.stem: In what sense does the acceptance-test template form a bridge toward BDD/Verification?

options.text: The scenario IS the specification of 'done', and automating it confirms the feature meets the requirement
It is only used for GUI replay

questions.explanation: The acceptance scenario specifies 'done'; turning it into an automated test is how Verification confirms the requirement. (Software Testing p.79)

questions.stem: Why does a green regression suite make Prefactoring and Postfactoring safe? Pick the best account.

options.text: Because refactoring never changes behaviour anyway
The suite proves the restructuring preserved behaviour, so cleaning code before/after a change is verifiably safe
Because assertions are disabled during refactoring

questions.explanation: Green tests are the safety net proving behaviour is preserved, which is what makes Prefactoring/Postfactoring safe. (Software Testing p.48, p.81)

questions.stem: A test uses a stub that returns a fixed clock time, then additionally calls verify() to check the stub was queried. What design smell does that reveal?

options.text: Over-specifying: a stub for a pure query shouldn't need interaction verification

questions.explanation: A pure query needs only a stub; adding verify() over-specifies and couples the test to interaction detail. (Software Testing p.59-60)

questions.stem: A team achieves 100% statement coverage but a bug hides in an untaken else-branch. Which criterion would have forced a test there?

options.text: Statement coverage, which already covers it
Branch coverage, since it requires both outcomes of the decision

questions.explanation: Branch coverage forces both true and false outcomes, including the else-branch statement coverage can miss; even so it isn't proof. (Software Testing p.11)

questions.stem: Why must the too-many-books behaviour be added as a NEW failing test rather than just editing the implementation?

options.text: TDD requires a failing test to justify and drive each new piece of production code
Because the old test must be deleted first
Because refactoring comes before any test

questions.explanation: TDD: you cannot write code you don't have a failing test for; the new test drives the new code. (Software Testing p.39, p.45)

questions.stem: A test asserts an exact log-message string and breaks when the wording is tweaked. Which sensitivity, and which TDD principle was likely violated?

options.text: Data sensitivity; sharing a database row
Behavior/interface sensitivity; over-specifying incidental behaviour beyond what the test needs
None; the test is correct to break

questions.explanation: Coupling to an incidental log string over-specifies behaviour, a fragile-test smell to avoid. (Software Testing p.26)

questions.stem: A test annotates fields with @Mock/@Spy/@InjectMocks but never calls MockitoAnnotations.initMocks(this). What breaks as a result?
questions.explanation: Without initMocks, the @Mock/@Spy/@InjectMocks fields remain null. (Software Testing p.65, p.75)

questions.stem: Why does the guide say only an automated regression suite makes continuous Postfactoring and Actualization safe?

options.text: It re-checks every change instantly, acting as the safety net for ongoing code changes

questions.explanation: Continuous changes are safe only because an automated suite re-verifies them on each change. (Software Testing p.81)

questions.stem: Testing sqrt over floats, one input deserves its own equivalence class as a special edge value. Which input is it?
questions.explanation: NaN is a special float edge value forming its own equivalence class. (Software Testing p.23)

questions.stem: What makes the guide read the p.8 testing-kinds diagram as two axes instead of a single flat list?

options.text: One axis is speed; the other is cost
One axis is the level (unit/integration/system); the other is knowledge (white-box vs black-box)
The axes are GUI vs CLI

questions.explanation: The map has a level axis and a white-box/black-box knowledge axis. (Software Testing p.8)

questions.stem: On the 'Equivalent Tests' slide, why is 'enqueue(7); x=dequeue(); if(x==7)' equivalent to the same code with a sleep(100) inserted?

options.text: The queue's contract is time-independent, so the sleep does not change the equivalence class

questions.explanation: The queue contract is time-independent, so adding a sleep keeps the inputs in the same equivalence class. (Software Testing p.12)

questions.stem: A team turns off assertions in production for speed, but a checkRep had a side effect tucked inside it. What follows, and which rule did they break?

options.text: Tests run faster with no downside; Rule 3 broken
The program won't compile; no rule broken
Behaviour changes when assertions are off; Rule 2 (no side effects) was broken

questions.explanation: A side-effecting assertion means disabling assertions changes behaviour, violating Rule 2. (Software Testing p.18)

questions.stem: Which combination correctly orders the doubles by increasing power/coupling with rationale?

options.text: stub (canned data) -> spy (real object, partial) -> mock (records+verifies)
mock -> spy -> stub, because mock is lightest
spy -> stub -> mock, because spy is most static
stub -> mock -> spy, because spy is most powerful

questions.explanation: Lightest/most static is the stub; spy wraps a real object; mock is most powerful/flexible. (Software Testing p.59-60, p.72)

questions.stem: A test pulls CPR '1234651234' from a shared live DB that another test keeps mutating, so it fails intermittently. Which test sensitivity is at work, and what is the cure?

options.text: Data sensitivity; own and control the test data

questions.explanation: Sharing mutable live data is data sensitivity; the cure is owning controlled test data. (Software Testing p.27, p.42)

questions.stem: A maintainer wants to verify a new release behaves like the old one with zero hand-written oracles. Which technique and why is it cheap?

options.text: Boundary-value analysis - it needs no oracle
Differential testing - the old release IS the oracle, so no expected values are authored
Equivalence partitioning - it reuses old data

questions.explanation: Differential testing uses the prior release as a free oracle, avoiding hand-written expected values. (Software Testing p.8)

questions.stem: Which statement best captures why a passing test suite is evidence, not proof?

options.text: Because tests are deleted after running
Because the compiler already proves correctness
Residual bugs can hide in untested paths, so a green suite shows working features but not correctness

questions.explanation: A green suite is evidence of working features; residual bugs may remain (presence not absence). (Software Testing p.6)

questions.stem: A bug only reproduces when tests run in a particular order because they share static state. Which testability checklist item, if followed, would have prevented it?

options.text: No swap of global variables (avoid hidden global state)

questions.explanation: Hidden global state causes order-dependence; the checklist's 'no global swaps' prevents it. (Software Testing p.14)

questions.stem: You want to confirm a method throws a particular exception type and check nothing further. Which JUnit idiom is the most concise fit, and why?

options.text: @Test(expected=...) because it needs no try/catch boilerplate

questions.explanation: When only the type matters, the annotation form is the most concise. (Software Testing p.55)

questions.stem: A test for a figure asserts both that move() returns the right new bounds AND that it called the canvas's repaint(). Which doubles/checks does this require, and is it ideal?

options.text: Only a stub; no verification needed
A spy on the bounds value
A mock with verify() for repaint plus an assertion on bounds; reserve verify for the interaction that matters

questions.explanation: Verifying the repaint interaction needs a mock+verify; asserting bounds is a value check - use verify only where the interaction matters. (Software Testing p.59)

questions.stem: A team's acceptance test is automated and written before the story is implemented. Which discipline does this mirror at the feature level?

options.text: The red-first TDD discipline applied to acceptance tests

questions.explanation: Agile acceptance tests are red-first (written before implementation), mirroring TDD at the feature level. (Software Testing p.78)

questions.stem: A maintainer claims branch coverage is unnecessary because statement coverage already ran every line. Counter with a concrete case.

options.text: A one-line if with no else: statement coverage runs the line, but branch coverage forces the false path too
Statement coverage already forces both outcomes
Branch coverage is weaker, so it adds nothing

questions.explanation: An if without an else can have its statement covered while the false branch is never taken - branch coverage forces it. (Software Testing p.11)

questions.stem: Two modules each pass unit tests but the product fails at their seam. Which testing LEVEL and which fault LOCATION are most relevant?
questions.explanation: The seam is integration-level, and an interface/spec mismatch is the likely fault location (Mars Orbiter). (Software Testing p.8-9)

questions.stem: A test depends on the system locale for number formatting and breaks abroad. Beyond naming the sensitivity, what is the structural cure?

options.text: Pin/control the locale (own the context) so the test is environment-independent

questions.explanation: Context sensitivity is cured by controlling the environment (e.g. pinning the locale). (Software Testing p.28)

questions.stem: A maintainer adds a defect test for borrowBook(null) AFTER the feature works. Which TDD/ideas-for-tests category and why add it post-hoc?

options.text: An acceptance test for the main scenario
A differential test against the old release
A defect test guarding against a possible defect at the null boundary; it pins down edge behaviour

questions.explanation: The null-input test is a defect/boundary test pinning down edge behaviour. (Software Testing p.47, p.40)

questions.stem: A reviewer sees verify(svc).hasOverdue(user) plus when(svc.hasOverdue(user)).thenReturn(false). What roles do these two lines play?

options.text: thenReturn stubs the query's answer; verify asserts the interaction (call) happened

questions.explanation: when().thenReturn() stubs the answer; verify() asserts the call occurred - stubbing vs verification. (Software Testing p.69, p.71)

questions.stem: Which best explains why the deck pairs 'test under the UI' with both fragile tests AND regression testing?

options.text: It only helps fragile tests, not regression
Moving logic under the UI makes tests stable enough to automate and re-run as regression
It only helps regression, not fragility

questions.explanation: Testing under the UI cures fragility and makes the suite automatable for regression. (Software Testing p.29, p.81)

questions.stem: Why does the guide say even a test that achieves branch coverage of borrowBook's guard still cannot prove borrowBook is correct?

options.text: Because branch coverage is impossible to reach
Presence-not-absence: untested inputs/paths may still harbour defects

questions.explanation: Covering the guard's branches still cannot prove correctness (Dijkstra). (Software Testing p.6)

questions.stem: A test instantiates LibraryApp, asserts not logged in, logs in, then asserts logged in. Which structural pattern is this?
questions.explanation: Asserting a precondition, performing the action, then asserting the postcondition is the Arrange-Act-Assert pattern. (Software Testing p.33)

questions.stem: A suite uses @RunWith(Suite.class) and @Suite.SuiteClasses({EnqueueTest.class, DequeueTest.class}). What artifact is this?
questions.explanation: This is a JUnit test suite that groups several test classes to run together. (Software Testing p.54)

questions.stem: A class has no checkRep and no assertions on its invariants. What is the most direct consequence for testing?

options.text: The class becomes impossible to compile
Invariant violations are not caught early and can surface far from the bug
Mockito can no longer mock it

questions.explanation: Assertions make code self-checking and fail early near the bug; without them, invariant violations slip through and surface late. (Software Testing p.20)

questions.stem: Code depends on wall-clock time without any clock abstraction. What testing consequence follows?
questions.explanation: Time-dependent code is context-sensitive and hard to test repeatably unless the clock is injected. (Software Testing p.32)

questions.stem: A test puts a side-effecting call inside an assert, and the team later disables assertions in production. What is the consequence?

options.text: Nothing changes; assertions never affect behaviour
The test suite runs faster with no downside
Program behaviour changes when assertions are off (Rule 2 violated)

questions.explanation: Rule 2 forbids side effects in assertions precisely because disabling them would then alter program behaviour. (Software Testing p.18)

questions.stem: The team relies only on manual tests. What is the consequence for regression testing?

options.text: They cannot run often enough, so changes go un-regression-checked
Manual tests give immediate feedback on every change

questions.explanation: Manual tests are expensive and can't run often, so they fail to provide regression coverage; automation enables it. (Software Testing p.81)

questions.stem: A developer writes more production code than the current failing test demands. According to TDD discipline, what is the consequence/remedy?

options.text: Untested speculative code exists; write another failing test to justify it
The extra code is automatically refactored
The test turns green without assertions

questions.explanation: TDD says implement only enough to pass; if more is needed, add another failing test rather than speculatively coding. (Software Testing p.39-40)

questions.stem: A test couples to one specific database row that another process can change. What is the consequence?

options.text: Intermittent failures unrelated to real defects (data sensitivity)

questions.explanation: Coupling to specific mutable data causes data-sensitivity fragility and intermittent false failures. (Software Testing p.27)

questions.stem: Global variables are swapped behind the scenes inside a module. What testing consequence does the deck warn about?
questions.explanation: Hidden global-state swaps make tests order-dependent and non-repeatable, which is why the testability checklist forbids them. (Software Testing p.14)

questions.stem: You need the STRONGEST coverage guarantee that every independent execution route was exercised. Which criterion do you target (accepting it is often infeasible)?
questions.explanation: Path coverage exercises every combination of branches and is the strongest (though often infeasible) criterion. (Software Testing p.13)

questions.stem: For a pure query dependency where you just need a fixed answer and will not assert on calls, which double is the BEST fit?
questions.explanation: A stub is the lightest double and the right choice for a pure query needing only a canned answer. (Software Testing p.60)

questions.stem: You want mostly-real behaviour from an object but need to override and verify just one method. Which double is BEST?
questions.explanation: A spy wraps a real object, delegating by default and letting you override/verify a few methods. (Software Testing p.72-73)

questions.stem: A figure depends on an interface-based storage layer you do not want to hit in a unit test. Which is the MOST appropriate technique?
questions.explanation: Interface-based collaborators are ideal for mocks/stubs so the unit test stays isolated. (Software Testing p.57-75)

questions.stem: You must do expensive shared setup (open a DB connection) ONCE for an entire test class. Which annotation is MOST appropriate?
questions.explanation: @BeforeClass is a static method run once before all tests in the class, ideal for expensive shared setup. (Software Testing p.52)

questions.stem: You want a fresh, invariant-checked fixture before EACH test method. Which annotation is MOST appropriate?
questions.explanation: @Before runs before each test method, giving each test a fresh fixture (e.g. a new CheckRepWrapper(Queue)). (Software Testing p.52)

questions.stem: You need to confirm a returned object reference is not null with the clearest possible failure message. Which assertion is BEST?
questions.explanation: Specialised assertions like assertNotNull give clearer failure messages than a plain assertTrue. (Software Testing p.53)

questions.stem: For verifying that the domain logic of a JHotDraw Swing feature works, which approach does TestLab1 most recommend?

options.text: JUnit 5 tests driving Swing widgets directly
A stress test of the rendering loop
JUnit 4 unit tests of the domain-logic methods under the UI

questions.explanation: TestLab1 says create JUnit 4 tests for the most important domain-logic methods (Swing/JUnit extensions favour JUnit 4). (TestLab1 p.1)

questions.stem: A teammate claims 'we hit 100% statement coverage, so the code is proven correct.' What is the correct rebuttal?

options.text: Coverage is not proof; residual bugs hide in untested paths and even full coverage can't prove absence of bugs
Correctness is guaranteed once assertions are enabled

questions.explanation: By Dijkstra, testing shows presence not absence of bugs; no coverage level proves correctness. (Software Testing p.6)

questions.stem: A developer says 'a red test always means the production code is buggy.' Why is this a misconception?

options.text: Red tests never indicate real bugs
The fault could be in the test, the spec, or the platform, not only the SUT
Only the compiler can cause red tests
Red tests are always caused by the oracle

questions.explanation: The fault-location decision tree considers SUT, acceptance test, specification, and platform before fixing anything. (Software Testing p.9)

questions.stem: A teammate insists 'assertions are a great way to handle invalid user input.' Bust the misconception.

options.text: Yes, that is their primary purpose
No - assertions are for 'should never happen' programmer bugs; bad input gets an exception
Assertions should validate every input by default

questions.explanation: Rule 1: assertions check programmer mistakes, not expected runtime errors; bad input is handled with exceptions. (Software Testing p.18)

questions.stem: A colleague says 'Java assertions run by default, so my checkRep is always active.' Correct them.

options.text: They are on by default, so the claim is right
Java assertions are OFF by default; you must enable them with -ea

questions.explanation: Java assertions are disabled by default and require -ea to run. (Software Testing p.16)

questions.stem: A teammate thinks 'a mock and a stub are the same thing, just different names.' Why is this a misconception?

options.text: A stub only returns canned data; a mock additionally records and verifies interactions
A stub records interactions; a mock does not
A mock is lighter than a stub

questions.explanation: Stubs supply predefined responses without verification; mocks verify the interaction. (Software Testing p.59-60)

questions.stem: A colleague says 'we should always use a mock with verify() for every dependency.' Why is this over-engineering?

options.text: Mocks are always cheaper than stubs
A stub suffices for a pure query; over-using mocks/verify over-specifies tests and makes them fragile

questions.explanation: Reserve mocks/verify for when the interaction is under test; a stub is enough for pure queries, and over-mocking causes fragility. (Software Testing p.59-60)

questions.stem: A developer adds 'assert 1+1==2;' to a method 'for safety.' Which rule does this violate and why?

options.text: Rule 3 (no silly assertions) - it adds noise, not value

questions.explanation: Rule 3 forbids trivial/silly assertions that add noise without value. (Software Testing p.18)

questions.stem: In the TDD cycle, what immediately follows writing a failing test and seeing it go red?

options.text: Write only enough production code to make it pass (green)

questions.explanation: After red comes green: write just enough code to pass, then refactor. (Software Testing p.39)

questions.stem: What is the mandatory THIRD step of the TDD cycle, after red and green?
questions.explanation: The third step is refactor: restructure without changing behaviour, enabled by the green tests. (Software Testing p.39, p.48)

questions.stem: Within the change mini-cycle, which phase is Lecture 7 (Testing) about, and what does it follow?

options.text: Verification, performed after the change is actualized to prove nothing broke

questions.explanation: Testing is the Verification phase: prove the change works and broke nothing, after the change is made. (Software Testing p.48, p.81)

questions.stem: In the basic test model, what is the correct flow of data?

options.text: test inputs -> SUT -> test outputs -> oracle judges 'Outputs OK?'

questions.explanation: Inputs feed the SUT, which produces outputs the oracle then judges pass/fail. (Software Testing p.7)

questions.stem: After Concept Location and Impact Analysis identify the modules a JHotDraw change touches, what does Lecture 7 say to do with those modules' tests?

options.text: Skip testing until the next release
(Re)write and run their unit tests, since the impact set maps onto the test levels

questions.explanation: The impacted modules are exactly the ones whose unit tests you must (re)write and run for Verification. (Software Testing p.8)

questions.stem: In the deck's TDD ideas-for-tests flow, a use-case scenario (missing function) leads to which kind of test?
questions.explanation: Use-case scenarios (missing functions) map to acceptance tests in the ideas-for-tests slide. (Software Testing p.40)

questions.stem: In the agile acceptance-test discipline, when is the automated acceptance test created relative to the user story?
questions.explanation: Agile acceptance tests are automatic and created before the user story is implemented. (Software Testing p.78)

questions.stem: JHotDraw is a Swing drawing editor. How does the deck's GUI advice say you should verify a figure's behaviour?

options.text: Drive every widget with click scripts as the primary suite
Only run system tests through the UI
Call the model/domain logic directly with JUnit instead of driving Swing widgets

questions.explanation: Test under the UI: verify figure/handle/command domain logic by calling the model directly, not Swing widgets. (Software Testing p.29)

questions.stem: Why are JHotDraw's collaborators described as ideal for mocks/stubs?

options.text: They are interface-based (it was built on design patterns), so dependencies can be substituted

questions.explanation: JHotDraw (co-authored by Gamma) is design-pattern, interface-based, which is ideal for substituting mocks/stubs. (Software Testing p.57-75)

questions.stem: Per TestLab1, which deliverable links Lecture 7 to the JHotDraw feature you chose?

options.text: Class-level unit tests of the feature's business functionality, documenting how the feature was verified
A manual checklist signed by the customer
A stress-test report of the rendering loop

questions.explanation: TestLab1: write unit tests of important business functionality and document how you verified your feature. (TestLab1 p.1)

questions.stem: Per the guide, what do assertions/checkRep look like once carried over into JHotDraw?

options.text: As invariant checks on things like a figure's bounds, a connection's endpoints, or z-order
As stress tests of the canvas
As differential tests against a reference editor

questions.explanation: JHotDraw invariants (figure bounds, connection endpoints, z-order) are encoded as assert checks per the test-invariants requirement. (Software Testing p.18-19)

questions.stem: What single artifact does the guide say you present as evidence for the Verification phase of a JHotDraw change?

options.text: A screenshot of the running editor
The documented suite of JUnit unit tests covering the changed domain logic

questions.explanation: The documented test suite of the changed feature is the Verification evidence. (TestLab1 p.1)

questions.stem: A JHotDraw figure depends on an undo manager you do not want to exercise in a unit test. What does the guide recommend?

options.text: Run the real undo manager and assert on the GUI
Replace it with a mock/stub via its interface

questions.explanation: Interface-based collaborators like an undo manager are substituted with mocks/stubs to isolate the unit. (Software Testing p.57-75)

questions.stem: On a fixed-size queue with max=2, trace enqueue(6); enqueue(7); enqueue(8); dequeue(); dequeue(); dequeue(). Which result tuple comes out?
questions.explanation: The third enqueue fails (queue full) and the third dequeue returns null (queue empty): true,true,false,6,7,null. (Software Testing p.11)

questions.stem: Read this snippet: Mockito.verify(test, Mockito.times(2)); What does it assert?

options.text: That the method was called exactly twice

questions.explanation: verify(mock, times(2)) asserts the method was invoked exactly two times. (Software Testing p.71)

questions.stem: Read this snippet: Mockito.verify(test).testing(Matchers.eq(12)); What does it assert?

options.text: That testing() was called (once) with the argument 12

questions.explanation: This verifies testing() was called with an argument equal to 12 (default once). (Software Testing p.71)

questions.stem: Given the spec line 'sqrt: F -> F+ U exception', what does it tell you must be tested?

options.text: That all floats return complex numbers
That only positive floats are valid
That negative floats may return a value OR throw an exception

questions.explanation: 'F -> F+ U exception' means float inputs (including negatives) map to a float or an exception, so both outcomes need tests. (Software Testing p.23)

questions.stem: Read: @Test(expected = MyException.class) public void testM(){ ... }. What does this test pass/fail on?

options.text: It passes only if the method body throws MyException
It passes only if the method returns MyException
It fails if any exception is thrown

questions.explanation: The annotation form passes only when the body throws the declared exception type. (Software Testing p.55)

questions.stem: Read: @Mock private ArticleDatabase database; ... verify(database).addListener(any(ArticleListener.class)); What is asserted?

options.text: That addListener was called once with some ArticleListener

questions.explanation: verify(database).addListener(any(...)) asserts addListener was called with any ArticleListener argument. (Software Testing p.75)

questions.stem: Read the assertion: assert result > 0; placed before 'return result;' in sqrt. What is its role?

options.text: A unit-test oracle external to the method
A postcondition invariant: a real square root must be positive

questions.explanation: It is an executable invariant/postcondition check that a real square root is positive. (Software Testing p.18)

questions.stem: Which best captures why differential testing is especially useful during maintenance?

options.text: It removes the need for any oracle
The previous release is a free oracle for the new version

questions.explanation: In maintenance the prior release serves as a free reference oracle for the new code. (Software Testing p.8)

questions.stem: A team wants immediate feedback that a code change introduced a bug. Which capability provides this?

options.text: Automated regression tests run on every change
Manual acceptance tests run after delivery

questions.explanation: Automated tests are cheap to run on every change, giving immediate regression feedback. (Software Testing p.81)

questions.stem: Which 'idea for tests' does the deck pair with 'possibility for defects (missing code)'?
questions.explanation: A possibility for defects (missing code) maps to defect tests. (Software Testing p.40)

questions.stem: The deck cites real-world assertion density. Which figure does it give?

options.text: About one assertion per 110 lines of code (GCC ~9,000, LLVM ~13,000)

questions.explanation: GCC ships ~9,000 and LLVM ~13,000 assertions, roughly one per 110 LOC. (Software Testing p.20)

questions.stem: Which best states when the deck says assertions SHOULD be disabled?

options.text: In a mission-critical stage where continuing is better than recovering by failing early

questions.explanation: Disable assertions only in a mission-critical stage where continuing beats failing early; otherwise prefer to fail early. (Software Testing p.21)

questions.stem: Why does the guide say machine floats (F) versus real numbers (R) matters for test design?

options.text: Float-specific edges like overflow, NaN, and -0.0 form their own test classes
Reals require more tests than floats

questions.explanation: Machine floats differ from real-number theory, so overflow/NaN/-0.0 are distinct test classes. (Software Testing p.23)

questions.stem: Which statement best describes record-and-play GUI testing and its named tool?

options.text: Inject faults into the OS; the deck names DbUnit
Capture and replay a GUI session; the deck names Sikuli Script
Mock the database; the deck names Mockito
Stress the server; the deck names CppUnit

questions.explanation: Record-and-play captures/replays GUI sessions; the deck names Sikuli Script for image-based GUI testing. (Software Testing p.30, p.56)

questions.stem: What does verifyNoMoreInteractions() check on a Mockito mock?

options.text: That the mock was never created
That no method other than those already verified was called
That the test has no assertions

questions.explanation: verifyNoMoreInteractions() asserts that no other (unverified) calls were made to the mock. (Software Testing p.74)

questions.stem: What does @InjectMocks attempt to do?

options.text: Constructor dependency injection of @Mock/@Spy fields into the object under test

questions.explanation: @InjectMocks wires the annotated mocks/spies into the object under test via constructor DI by type. (Software Testing p.74)

questions.stem: In the Spring + JUnit example, by what means does the test get hold of the DataModelService?

options.text: It is injected by Spring via @Autowired @Qualifier
It is read from a file

questions.explanation: Spring wires the bean via @Autowired/@Qualifier and the test asserts on the injected instance. (Software Testing p.76)

questions.stem: Which xUnit-family member does the deck name for database fixture management?
questions.explanation: DbUnit manages database fixtures in the xUnit family. (Software Testing p.56)

questions.stem: Which acceptance-test template fields appear in the deck's Login Admin example?

options.text: Name, Actor, Precondition, Main scenario, Alternative scenarios, Postcondition

questions.explanation: The template lists Name, Actor, Precondition, Main scenario, Alternative scenarios, and Postcondition. (Software Testing p.79)

questions.stem: What is the closing 'tester's mindset' contrast on the deck's final slide?

options.text: Developer wants it to fail; tester wants it to succeed
Developer wants the code to succeed; tester wants it to fail; the hybrid holds both

questions.explanation: Developer wants success, tester wants failure, and the ideal engineer is the hybrid. (Software Testing p.83)

questions.stem: In TestLab1's definition, what is a 'unit' in object-oriented programming?

options.text: Often an entire interface such as a class, but possibly an individual method
Only a single line of code

questions.explanation: TestLab1: a unit is often an entire interface such as a class, but could be an individual method. (TestLab1 p.1)

questions.stem: Why does the deck prefer specialised assertions like assertNull over a plain assertTrue(x == null)?
questions.explanation: Specialised assertions give clearer failure messages, aiding diagnosis. (Software Testing p.53)

questions.stem: The queue resets its tail to 0 with 'if (tail == max) tail = 0;'. Which boundary condition does that line create?

options.text: The wrap-around boundary of the ring buffer

questions.explanation: This line creates the wrap-around boundary where tail resets to 0. (Software Testing p.11)

questions.stem: Against the fragile-test problem, which single cross-cutting cure does the deck put forward?

options.text: Testing under the UI (test domain logic directly)

questions.explanation: The deck's cross-cutting cure is to test under the UI, converting brittle manual tests into stable automatic ones. (Software Testing p.29)

questions.stem: How does the data-sensitivity cure relate to TestLab1's 'Testdata' sections?

options.text: They own and control test data (e.g. CPR '1234651234') to avoid coupling to shared state
They share one live database row

questions.explanation: Owning controlled test data (the Testdata sections) is the cure for data sensitivity. (Software Testing p.27, p.42)

questions.stem: Which concept does a CI pipeline that re-runs the full suite on every commit most directly implement?
questions.explanation: Re-running the suite on every commit is regression testing, the heartbeat of Verification/CI. (Software Testing p.81)

questions.stem: Which code change is the 'green' implementation that satisfies the alternative too-many-books scenario?

options.text: Add 'if (borrowedBooks.size() >= 10) throw new TooManyBooksException();' before adding

questions.explanation: The alternative scenario is satisfied by guarding with size>=10 and throwing TooManyBooksException. (Software Testing p.46)

questions.stem: What does the final Borrow-Book defect test (book==null) check?

options.text: That the book list is cleared
That the borrowed count is unchanged when book is null

questions.explanation: The null defect test checks that borrowing null does not change the borrowed count. (Software Testing p.47)

questions.stem: Boiled down to one rule, what does Test-Driven Development demand?

options.text: Write the test before the implementation
Write the implementation, then the test
Skip tests if coverage is high

questions.explanation: TDD's defining rule is to write the test before the implementation. (Software Testing p.35)

questions.stem: Within TDD's red-green-refactor loop, what exactly happens in the 'green' step?

options.text: Write only enough production code to make the failing test pass
Restructure the code without changing behaviour

questions.explanation: Green = write just enough production code to pass, no more. (Software Testing p.39)

questions.stem: How does the deck define 'Real TDD' versus 'Traditional Testing'?

options.text: Real TDD writes code first; Traditional tests first
Real TDD writes the test strictly first; Traditional writes code first and tests after

questions.explanation: Real TDD is test-strictly-first; Traditional is code-first/test-after. (Software Testing p.36, p.38)

questions.stem: Interface sensitivity over-couples a test to what?

options.text: The shape of the API (signatures, parameter order)

questions.explanation: Interface sensitivity couples to API shape; renames/reorders break tests though behaviour is unchanged. (Software Testing p.25)

questions.stem: Context sensitivity over-couples a test to what?

options.text: Environment such as time, locale, filesystem, or ordering

questions.explanation: Context sensitivity couples to environment: time, locale, FS, network, ordering. (Software Testing p.28)

questions.stem: Data sensitivity over-couples a test to what?

options.text: Specific data/state such as a fixed database row or file

questions.explanation: Data sensitivity couples to specific data/state; changing the fixture breaks the test. (Software Testing p.27)

questions.stem: The testability checklist credits two practices with keeping code readable and thus testable. Which pair?
questions.explanation: Clean code and refactoring make code readable and testable. (Software Testing p.14)

questions.stem: Why does the guide say the queue tuple 'true,true,false,6,7,null' is significant?

options.text: It proves the queue is correct
It shows path coverage is complete
Every value in it is a boundary outcome

questions.explanation: Each value (full-return false, empty-return null, FIFO order) is a boundary outcome. (Software Testing p.11)

questions.stem: What does branch coverage specifically force in the queue's enqueue method?

options.text: Both a test where enqueue returns false (full) and one where it returns true

questions.explanation: Branch coverage forces both outcomes: enqueue returning true and returning false. (Software Testing p.11)

questions.stem: What does a specification define for each input domain?
questions.explanation: A specification maps each input domain to its expected output range. (Software Testing p.23)

questions.stem: Which fault location should you consider FIRST and is the most common when a test fails?
questions.explanation: The SUT is the first and most common candidate in the fault-location decision tree. (Software Testing p.9)

questions.stem: The Mars Climate Orbiter serves as the stock illustration of a fault sitting in which location?
questions.explanation: Mars Orbiter was a spec/interface unit mismatch (metric vs English). (Software Testing p.9)

questions.stem: Which two idioms does the deck give for testing that a method throws an exception?
questions.explanation: The two exception-test idioms are try/fail/catch and the @Test(expected=...) annotation. (Software Testing p.55)

questions.stem: What is Mockito, per the deck?

options.text: A popular mock framework used with JUnit to create and configure mock objects

questions.explanation: Mockito is a mock framework used with JUnit to create/configure mocks. (Software Testing p.58)

questions.stem: When using @Mock, what call initializes the annotated fields?
questions.explanation: @Mock fields are initialized by MockitoAnnotations.initMocks(this). (Software Testing p.65)

questions.stem: By default, what happens to an unstubbed call on a Mockito spy?

options.text: It is delegated to the real object's method

questions.explanation: A spy delegates unspecified calls to the real wrapped object. (Software Testing p.73)

questions.stem: What does the deck pinpoint as the leftover difficulty in regression testing, and what fix does it offer?

options.text: The UI is harder to regression-test; the solution is to test under the UI
Assertions are the difficulty; disable them
Mocks are the difficulty; remove them
Coverage is the difficulty; ignore it

questions.explanation: The UI is the hard part of regression; the cure is again testing under the UI. (Software Testing p.81)

questions.stem: Why does the guide call the oracle choice 'half the art of testing'?

options.text: A correct, cheap oracle is what actually decides pass/fail; a bad oracle invalidates the test

questions.explanation: The oracle judges correctness, so choosing a correct, cheap one is central to a meaningful test. (Software Testing p.7)

questions.stem: Weighing manual against automated tests, which claim does the deck endorse?

options.text: Manual tests give immediate regression feedback
Automated tests are more expensive to run
Automated tests can run as soon as something changes; manual tests can't be run often

questions.explanation: Automated tests run on every change (immediate feedback); manual tests are too expensive to run often. (Software Testing p.81)

questions.stem: Which phase of Rajlich's change mini-cycle does Lecture 7 cover?
questions.explanation: Lecture 7 (testing) is the Verification phase. (Software Testing p.48)

questions.stem: The Equivalent Tests slide raises 'Large Integers?' as a partitioning question. It partitions which of the test spaces?
questions.explanation: 'Large Integers?' is an output-space partition near the representable-range edge. (Software Testing p.12)

questions.stem: Equivalence partitioning directly addresses which earlier theoretical problem?

options.text: The impossibility of a perfect test suite over an infinite domain

questions.explanation: Partitioning gives adequate finite coverage of an infinite domain, addressing the impossibility result. (Software Testing p.12, p.5)

questions.stem: Why do the deck's reasons say assertions help debugging?

options.text: They make code fail early, closer to the bug

questions.explanation: Assertions make code fail early near the bug, aiding diagnosis. (Software Testing p.20)

questions.stem: How can both modules pass their unit tests yet the integrated system still fail, as the Mars Orbiter teaches?

options.text: The modules disagree on a spec/interface detail (units) each side handles consistently

questions.explanation: Each module is internally consistent but they disagree on the interface contract (units), a spec defect. (Software Testing p.9)

questions.stem: Renaming a public method during refactoring makes many tests fail to compile even though behaviour is identical. Which test sensitivity is this, and what is the takeaway?

options.text: Interface sensitivity; test through stable interfaces

questions.explanation: Coupling to method names is interface sensitivity; mitigate by testing through stable interfaces. (Software Testing p.25)

questions.stem: According to TestLab1, what should an assertion do when it fails?
questions.explanation: TestLab1: an assertion should stop the program, whereas an exception lets it continue. (TestLab1 p.1)

questions.stem: What does the SUT acronym stand for?
questions.explanation: SUT = Software Under Test. (Software Testing p.7)

questions.stem: What is a defect test, per the deck's ideas-for-tests?

options.text: An acceptance test from a use case
A test pinning down a possible defect / over-written code

questions.explanation: A defect test targets a possibility for defects (you wrote more code than the test demanded). (Software Testing p.40)

questions.stem: Mapping 'test under the UI' onto Clean Architecture for JHotDraw, what does it amount to?

options.text: Keep business rules independent of the UI so they are unit-testable
Put all logic in the widgets

questions.explanation: Clean Architecture keeps business rules UI-independent, enabling under-the-UI unit testing. (Software Testing p.29)

questions.stem: Which is the FIRST (most common) fault location to consider, and the complete ordered set?

options.text: Platform, then spec, then test, then SUT
SUT, then acceptance test, then specification, then platform
Spec, then SUT, then platform, then test
Test, then platform, then SUT, then spec

questions.explanation: The decision tree order is SUT, acceptance test, specification, platform. (Software Testing p.9)

questions.stem: A CI pipeline re-runs the suite on every commit. Which is the BEST description of what it implements and why it matters?

options.text: Stress testing - it loads the server
Regression testing - it catches newly introduced breakage immediately
